Name: Seraphim
Also Known As: Burning Ones; Six-Winged Seraphim; Highest Order of Angels; Seraph; Serafim; Serafimy (Slavic)
Scripture References: Isaiah 6:1–7; Isaiah 6:6–7; Revelation 4:8; Hebrews 12:22–24; Daniel 7:9–10
Brief: The highest of the nine angelic orders — the six-winged “burning ones” of Isaiah’s vision who surround the throne of God, crying “Holy, Holy, Holy.” Their fire signifies not physical flame but spiritual ardor, purity, and nearness to God, and their unceasing praise is echoed in the Sanctus of the Divine Liturgy.
Tags: Heavenly Hosts; Angelic Orders; Seraphim; Nine Orders of Angels; Isaiah; Heavenly Worship; Divine Liturgy; Bodiless Powers; Celestial Hierarchy; Throne of God; Old Testament; Patristics; Iconography; Salvation History; Church Fathers
Notes: No deuterocanonical book names the Seraphim, though heavenly worship and angelic attendance appear in Tobit, the Wisdom of Solomon, and 2 Maccabees. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071). Related orders (Cherubim, Thrones, …) to be cross-linked in Related Beings as they are added.
Sources: Isaiah 6:1–7; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Jean Daniélou, The Angels and Their Mission; Vladimir Lossky, The Mystical Theology of the Eastern Church

Name: Cherubim
Also Known As: Keruvim; Kerubim; Cherubs; Cherub; Cherubic Order
Scripture References: Genesis 3:24; Exodus 25:18–22; Numbers 7:89; 1 Samuel 4:4; 2 Samuel 22:11; 1 Kings 6–8; Psalm 18:10; Psalm 80:1; Psalm 99:1; Ezekiel 1; Ezekiel 10; Ezekiel 41:18–25; Hebrews 9:5; Revelation 4:6–8
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The second of the nine angelic orders — the many-eyed, many-winged guardians of God’s presence, who barred the way to Eden, overshadowed the Ark of the Covenant, and bore the divine chariot-throne of Ezekiel’s vision. Patristic tradition links them to divine wisdom, knowledge, and the contemplation of God’s mysteries; the faithful join their worship in the Cherubic Hymn of the Divine Liturgy.
Tags: Cherubim; Angelic Orders; Heavenly Hosts; Nine Orders of Angels; Garden of Eden; Ark of the Covenant; Ezekiel; Heavenly Worship; Divine Liturgy; Bodiless Powers; Old Testament; New Testament; Temple Worship; Throne of God; Paradise; Iconography; Church Fathers; Salvation History; Celestial Hierarchy
Notes: No deuterocanonical text develops a Cherubim theology comparable to Ezekiel, though heavenly worship appears in Tobit, the Wisdom of Solomon, and 2 Maccabees. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Genesis 3:24; Exodus 25–37; 1 Kings 6–8; Ezekiel 1, 10, 41; Hebrews 9:5; Revelation 4–5; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Divine Liturgy of St. John Chrysostom; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Vladimir Lossky, The Mystical Theology of the Eastern Church; Jean Daniélou, The Angels and Their Mission

Name: Thrones
Also Known As: Holy Thrones; Angelic Thrones; Third Angelic Rank; Ophanim; Wheels; Thronoi; Throni; Ofanim
Scripture References: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:20–21; 1 Peter 3:22; Daniel 7:9–10; Revelation 4:2–11; Revelation 20:4
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The third of the nine angelic orders and the last of the highest triad, named among the invisible powers created “through Him and for Him” in Colossians 1:16. The Dionysian tradition reads their name symbolically — signifying stability, purity, and receptivity to God rather than any bodily seat — and later iconography associates them with the fiery, many-eyed wheels (Ophanim) of Ezekiel’s chariot-vision.
Tags: Heavenly Hosts; Angelic Orders; Thrones; Nine Orders of Angels; Bodiless Powers; Ophanim; Invisible Creation; New Testament; Colossians; Heavenly Worship; Celestial Hierarchy; Patristics; Divine Liturgy; Iconography; Creation; Salvation History; Synaxis of the Archangels
Notes: The Thrones-as-angelic-order rests chiefly on Colossians 1:16; the nine-rank classification is a patristic and liturgical synthesis, not a single scriptural statement. In the deuterocanon “thrones” (Wisdom of Solomon 7:8; Sirach 47:11) denotes royal seats, not the angelic order. The identification of the Thrones with the Ophanim / “wheels” of Ezekiel is a later interpretive and iconographic tradition, not a direct scriptural equation. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:20–21; 1 Peter 3:22; Daniel 7:9–10; Revelation 4:2–11; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Colossians; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Antiochian Orthodox Christian Archdiocese

Name: Dominions
Also Known As: Lordships; Kyriotetes; Holy Dominions; Angelic Dominions; Dominion; Dominationes
Scripture References: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:21; Colossians 2:10; Romans 8:38; 1 Peter 3:22; Daniel 7:9–10; Psalm 103:20–21; 1 Kings 22:19
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The fourth of the nine angelic orders and the first of the middle triad, named among the invisible powers created “through Him and for Him” in Colossians 1:16. Their name signifies lordship or governance: patristic tradition associates them with freedom from earthly passion, stability in divine service, and the orderly transmission of God’s providence through the heavenly hierarchy.
Tags: Dominions; Angelic Orders; Heavenly Hosts; Nine Orders of Angels; Bodiless Powers; Celestial Hierarchy; Divine Liturgy; Church Fathers; Invisible Creation; Heavenly Worship; Salvation History; Patristics; New Testament; Colossians; Byzantine Theology; Angelology; Synaxis of the Archangels
Notes: The Dominions rest scripturally on Colossians 1:16 and the Pauline lists of invisible powers; the ninefold ranking is a patristic synthesis, not a single scriptural statement. No deuterocanonical text names them, though the Wisdom of Solomon, Tobit, and Sirach speak of angels and divine governance. Also called Lordships (Greek Kyriotētes). Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:21; Colossians 2:10; Romans 8:38; 1 Peter 3:22; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Colossians; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Jean Daniélou, The Angels and Their Mission; Vladimir Lossky, The Mystical Theology of the Eastern Church

Name: Virtues
Also Known As: Holy Virtues; Dynameis; Angelic Virtues; Virtue; Dynamis; Virtutes
Scripture References: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:21; Ephesians 3:10; Romans 8:38; 1 Peter 3:22; Psalm 103:20–21; Daniel 7:10; 1 Kings 22:19
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The fifth of the nine angelic orders and the second of the middle triad, below the Dominions and above the Powers. Their Greek name, Dynameis (“powers” or “mighty works”), links them in patristic tradition to the operation of divine power and to strength, steadfastness, and the carrying-out of God’s will throughout creation.
Tags: Virtues; Angelic Orders; Heavenly Hosts; Nine Orders of Angels; Bodiless Powers; Celestial Hierarchy; Divine Power; Heavenly Worship; Church Fathers; Invisible Creation; Byzantine Theology; Salvation History; Patristics; New Testament; Divine Providence; Angelology; Synaxis of the Archangels
Notes: The Virtues rest scripturally on the Pauline lists of invisible powers (Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:21); their Greek name Dynameis (“powers” / “mighty works”) is the same word used for miracles in the New Testament, which shaped their later association with divine power. Not to be confused with the sixth rank, the Powers (Greek Exousiai). The ninefold ranking is a patristic synthesis, not an explicit biblical system. No deuterocanonical text names them. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Colossians 1:16; Ephesians 1:21; Ephesians 3:10; Romans 8:38; 1 Peter 3:22; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Ephesians and Colossians; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Jean Daniélou, The Angels and Their Mission; Vladimir Lossky, The Mystical Theology of the Eastern Church

Name: Powers
Also Known As: Holy Powers; Angelic Powers; Heavenly Powers; Exousiai; Authorities; Power
Scripture References: Colossians 1:16; Colossians 2:10; Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 1:20–21; Ephesians 3:10; Ephesians 6:12; Romans 8:38–39; 1 Peter 3:22; 1 Corinthians 15:24; Psalm 103:20–21; Daniel 7:9–10; 1 Kings 22:19
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The sixth of the nine angelic orders and the last of the middle triad, below the Virtues and above the Principalities. Named among the invisible powers created “through Him and for Him” in Colossians 1:16, Orthodox synaxarial tradition associates them with strength, unhesitating obedience, and the working of miracles. Scripture uses “powers” in more than one sense, so the holy order is carefully distinguished from the hostile spiritual powers that Christ disarms.
Tags: Powers; Angelic Orders; Heavenly Hosts; Nine Orders of Angels; Bodiless Powers; Celestial Hierarchy; Invisible Creation; Divine Liturgy; Synaxis of the Archangels; New Testament; Colossians; Ephesians; Spiritual Warfare; Heavenly Worship; Patristics; Church Fathers; Angelology; Salvation History; Divine Providence
Notes: Scripture uses “powers” in more than one sense: some principalities and powers belong to the holy created order and are subject to Christ (Colossians 1:16; 1 Peter 3:22), while others are hostile spiritual forces that Christ disarms (Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 6:12). The holy angelic order called Powers must be distinguished from these fallen powers, and from the fifth rank, the Virtues (Greek Dynameis); the Greek name of THIS order is Exousiai (“authorities”). The ninefold ranking is a patristic and liturgical synthesis, not a single scriptural list. No deuterocanonical text names the Powers, though Tobit 12:15, 2 Maccabees 3:24–26, and the Wisdom of Solomon speak of angels more broadly. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Colossians 1:16; Colossians 2:10; Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 1:20–21; Ephesians 3:10; Ephesians 6:12; Romans 8:38–39; 1 Peter 3:22; 1 Corinthians 15:24; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Colossians; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA

Name: Principalities
Also Known As: Holy Principalities; Angelic Principalities; Rulers; Archai; Princes; Principality
Scripture References: Colossians 1:16; Colossians 2:10; Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 1:20–21; Ephesians 3:10; Ephesians 6:12; Romans 8:38–39; 1 Corinthians 15:24; 1 Peter 3:22; Daniel 10:13; Daniel 10:20–21; Daniel 12:1; Psalm 103:20–21; 1 Kings 22:19
Extra-Biblical Sources: 1 Enoch; Jubilees; 2 Esdras (4 Ezra); Testament literature
Brief: The seventh of the nine angelic orders and the first of the lowest triad, above the Archangels and Angels. Named among the invisible powers created “through Him and for Him” in Colossians 1:16, Orthodox synaxarial tradition assigns them oversight of the lower angels and the guardianship of lands, nations, and peoples, instructing earthly rulers to govern for God. As with “powers,” the word “principalities” is used in Scripture for holy angels, earthly rulers, and hostile spirits alike, so the holy order is distinguished by context.
Tags: Principalities; Angelic Orders; Heavenly Hosts; Nine Orders of Angels; Bodiless Powers; Celestial Hierarchy; Invisible Creation; Divine Liturgy; Synaxis of the Archangels; New Testament; Colossians; Ephesians; Spiritual Warfare; Heavenly Worship; Patristics; Church Fathers; Angelology; Nations; Authority; Governance; Salvation History
Notes: The Greek archai (“rulers” / “beginnings”) is used in Scripture for holy angels, for earthly rulers (Titus 3:1), and for hostile spiritual powers (Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 6:12), so the holy angelic order called Principalities is distinguished from these by context. Daniel’s angelic “princes” of the nations (Daniel 10:13, 20–21; 12:1) supply the background for the later association of Principalities with the oversight of peoples; the specific ministry (protecting lands and nations, guiding rulers) is liturgical-synaxarial interpretation, not a direct scriptural narrative. The ninefold ranking is a patristic synthesis. No deuterocanonical text names them, though Tobit 12:15, 2 Maccabees 3:24–26, and the Wisdom of Solomon speak of angels and governance more broadly. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Colossians 1:16; Colossians 2:10; Colossians 2:15; Ephesians 1:20–21; Ephesians 3:10; Ephesians 6:12; Romans 8:38–39; 1 Corinthians 15:24; 1 Peter 3:22; Daniel 10; Daniel 12:1; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Colossians; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA

Name: Archangels
Also Known As: Holy Archangels; Chief Angels; Chief Messengers; Archistrategoi; Archangeloi
Scripture References: Daniel 8:15–26; Daniel 9:20–27; Daniel 10:13; Daniel 10:21; Daniel 12:1; Luke 1:11–20; Luke 1:26–38; Matthew 1:20–24; Matthew 2:13; Matthew 2:19–20; Matthew 28:2–7; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Jude 1:9; Revelation 8:2; Revelation 12:7–9
Brief: The eighth of the nine angelic orders and the chief messengers of God — the “ruling angels” who bear His greatest tidings to humanity. Orthodox tradition names Michael, Gabriel, Raphael, Uriel, Selaphiel, Jegudiel, and Barachiel (and sometimes Jeremiel) among them: Michael leads the heavenly hosts, Gabriel announced the Incarnation to the Theotokos, and Raphael, in Tobit, brought healing and guidance. Only Michael is directly called “the archangel” in Scripture (Jude 1:9).
Tags: Archangels; Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Nine Orders of Angels; Divine Messenger; Spiritual Warfare; Protection; Healing; Annunciation; Synaxis of the Archangels; Old Testament; New Testament; Deuterocanonical Scripture; Iconography; Salvation History; Heavenly Worship; Michael; Gabriel; Raphael
Notes: The word “archangel” (“chief messenger,” Greek archē + angelos) appears explicitly only in the New Testament (Jude 1:9, of Michael; 1 Thessalonians 4:16). Michael is named in canonical Scripture (Daniel, Jude, Revelation) and Gabriel in Daniel and Luke; Raphael is named in the deuterocanonical Tobit as one of the seven who stand before God. Uriel, Selaphiel, Jegudiel, Barachiel, and sometimes Jeremiel are known chiefly from later Orthodox liturgical tradition. The Church venerates the archangels but does not worship them (the Council of Laodicea condemned the worship of angels as gods). Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Daniel 8–12; Tobit 5–12; Luke 1:11–38; Matthew 1–2; Matthew 28:2–7; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Jude 1:9; Revelation 12:7–9; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on the Pauline Epistles; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America; Ukrainian Orthodox Church of the USA

Name: Angels
Also Known As: Holy Angels; Ninth Order of Angels; Angeloi; Angel; Ministering Spirits
Scripture References: Genesis 16:7–13; Genesis 22:11–18; Genesis 28:12; Genesis 32:1–2; Exodus 23:20–23; Numbers 22:22–35; Judges 6:11–24; Judges 13:3–21; 1 Kings 19:5–8; Daniel 6:22; Matthew 1:20–24; Matthew 4:11; Matthew 18:10; Matthew 28:2–7; Luke 2:8–14; Luke 22:43; Acts 5:19; Acts 8:26; Acts 12:7–11; Acts 12:15; Hebrews 1:14
Brief: The ninth and lowest of the angelic orders — nearest to humanity and most directly at work in daily life. From the Greek angelos (“messenger”), they guide, protect, strengthen, and carry God’s tidings to individuals and communities, and Orthodox tradition associates the guardian angels with this order. Being closest to us, the many unnamed angels of Scripture — who freed Peter, strengthened Elijah and Christ, and announced the Nativity — are traditionally reckoned among them.
Tags: Angels; Ninth Order; Guardian Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Angelic Hierarchy; Divine Messengers; Spiritual Protection; Ministering Spirits; Heavenly Worship; Invisible Creation; Church Fathers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Salvation History; Old Testament; New Testament; Deuterocanonical Scripture; Guardian Angel Tradition; Iconography
Notes: “Angel” (Greek angelos, “messenger”) is used broadly in Scripture for all heavenly beings, and more narrowly in the Dionysian hierarchy for this ninth and lowest order, nearest to humanity. Guardian angels are associated with this rank by patristic interpretation and liturgical tradition (Matthew 18:10; Acts 12:15) rather than by an explicit scriptural ranking. Many unnamed angelic appearances — the angel who freed Peter, strengthened Elijah and Christ, or announced the Nativity — are traditionally reckoned to this order because it stands closest to the visible world. The ninefold ranking itself is a patristic and liturgical synthesis. Commemorated Nov 8 (Julian; Nov 21 Gregorian) at the Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers (FF-0071).
Sources: Genesis 16, 22, 28, 32; Exodus 23:20–23; Judges 6, 13; 1 Kings 19:5–8; Daniel 6:22; Tobit 5–12; Matthew 18:10; Luke 2:8–14; Luke 22:43; Acts 12:7–11; Hebrews 1:14; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies; St. Gregory the Dialogist, Homilies on the Gospels; Menaion (Nov 8 Synaxis); Orthodox Church in America

Name: Archangel Michael
Also Known As: Michael; Mikha'el; Michael the Archangel; Archistrategos; Taxiarch; Holy Archangel Michael
Scripture References: Daniel 10:13; Daniel 10:21; Daniel 12:1; Jude 1:9; Revelation 12:7–9; 1 Thessalonians 4:16
Brief: The chief of the archangels and commander (archistrategos) of the heavenly hosts, whose name means “Who is like God?” He is the great defender against evil and protector of God’s people, who cast down the dragon (Revelation 12) and is the only being directly called “the archangel” in Scripture (Jude 1:9).
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Michael; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Spiritual Warfare; Protection; Divine Messenger; Commander of the Hosts; Synaxis of the Archangels; Old Testament; New Testament; Revelation; Iconography
Notes: Chief (archistrategos) of the heavenly hosts; name = “Who is like God?” Commemorated Nov 8 (Synaxis of the Bodiless Powers) and Sep 6 (Miracle at Chonae, FF-0073). The only being directly called “the archangel” in Scripture (Jude 1:9).
Sources: Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1; Jude 1:9; Revelation 12:7–9; 1 Thessalonians 4:16; Menaion (Nov 8; Sep 6); Orthodox Church in America

Name: Archangel Gabriel
Also Known As: Gabriel; Gavriil; Gabriel the Archangel; Holy Archangel Gabriel
Scripture References: Daniel 8:16; Daniel 9:21; Luke 1:11–20; Luke 1:26–38
Brief: The archangel of divine announcements, whose name means “God is my strength.” He interpreted the visions of Daniel, foretold the birth of John the Forerunner, and above all announced the Incarnation to the Most Holy Theotokos (Luke 1).
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Gabriel; Annunciation; Divine Messenger; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Theotokos; Incarnation; Synaxis of the Archangels; Old Testament; New Testament; Iconography
Notes: Name = “God is my strength.” The archangel of the Annunciation. Commemorated Nov 8, and at his own synaxes on Mar 26 (FF-0072, the day after the Annunciation) and Jul 13.
Sources: Daniel 8:16; 9:21; Luke 1:11–20, 26–38; Menaion (Nov 8; Mar 26; Jul 13); Orthodox Church in America

Name: Archangel Raphael
Also Known As: Raphael; Rafael; Raphael the Archangel; Holy Archangel Raphael
Brief: The archangel of healing, whose name means “God heals.” In the deuterocanonical book of Tobit he accompanies and guides Tobias, heals the blindness of Tobit, and reveals himself as one of the seven holy angels who stand before God.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Raphael; Healing; Guidance; Deuterocanonical Scripture; Tobit; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Iconography
Notes: Name = “God heals.” Named in the deuterocanonical Tobit as one of the seven who stand before God (Tobit 12:15). Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: Tobit 3:16–17; 5–12; 12:15; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Church in America

Name: Archangel Uriel
Also Known As: Uriel; Ouriel; Uri'el; Holy Archangel Uriel
Brief: One of the archangels received in Orthodox tradition, whose name means “Fire of God” or “Light of God.” He is associated with divine light, wisdom, and the illumination of the mind.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Uriel; Divine Light; Wisdom; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Traditional; Iconography
Notes: Name = “Fire/Light of God.” Received among the archangels in Orthodox tradition; his name appears in 2 Esdras (4 Ezra) and 1 Enoch rather than in the received canon. Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: 2 Esdras 4; 1 Enoch 20; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox liturgical tradition

Name: Archangel Selaphiel
Also Known As: Selaphiel; Sealtiel; Salathiel; Holy Archangel Selaphiel
Brief: The archangel of prayer, whose name means “Prayer of God.” He is depicted with hands folded upon his breast in supplication and is invoked as a helper and teacher of prayer.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Selaphiel; Prayer; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Traditional; Iconography
Notes: Name = “Prayer of God.” The archangel of prayer, received in Orthodox liturgical and iconographic tradition. Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox liturgical tradition; traditional iconography

Name: Archangel Jegudiel
Also Known As: Jegudiel; Jehudiel; Holy Archangel Jegudiel
Brief: The archangel who encourages and rewards those who labor for the glory of God, whose name means “Glorifier of God.” He is shown bearing a golden crown and a whip — reward for the diligent, admonition for the slothful.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Jegudiel; Labor for God; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Traditional; Iconography
Notes: Name = “Glorifier of God.” Encourages those who labor for God’s glory; shown with a crown and a whip. Received in Orthodox tradition. Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox liturgical tradition; traditional iconography

Name: Archangel Barachiel
Also Known As: Barachiel; Barakiel; Holy Archangel Barachiel
Brief: The archangel of God’s blessings, whose name means “Blessing of God.” He is the bestower of heaven’s benedictions upon the faithful, often depicted scattering rose petals from his garments.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Barachiel; Blessings; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Traditional; Iconography
Notes: Name = “Blessing of God.” The bestower of God’s blessings, often shown scattering rose petals. Received in Orthodox tradition. Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox liturgical tradition; traditional iconography

Name: Archangel Jeremiel
Also Known As: Jeremiel; Jerahmeel; Jeremeel; Holy Archangel Jeremiel
Brief: An archangel sometimes numbered among the eight, whose name means “Exaltation of God” (or “God’s mercy,” as Jerahmeel). Named in 2 Esdras, he is associated with the raising up of hope and with divine visions.
Tags: Archangels; Named Angels; Jeremiel; Hope; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Synaxis of the Archangels; Traditional; Iconography
Notes: Name = “Exaltation of God” (or “God’s mercy,” as Jerahmeel). Sometimes numbered as the eighth archangel; named in 2 Esdras (4 Ezra). Associated with the raising up of hope. Commemorated Nov 8.
Sources: 2 Esdras 4:36; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox liturgical tradition

Name: Guardian Angels
Also Known As: Guardian Angel; Protecting Angel; Personal Angel; Angelic Guardian; Angel of Protection
Scripture References: Matthew 18:10; Psalm 90(91):11–12; Acts 12:15; Hebrews 1:14; Genesis 48:16; Daniel 6:22; Luke 16:22
Brief: The angels whom God appoints to protect, guide, and spiritually care for individual human beings. Rooted in Christ’s words that the little ones’ “angels always behold the face of My Father” (Matthew 18:10), the Orthodox teaching holds that God assigns an angel to watch over each of the faithful — traditionally numbered among the Angels, the ninth rank and the order nearest to humanity.
Tags: Guardian Angels; Angels; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Protection; Guidance; Spiritual Warfare; Prayer; Divine Providence; Ninth Rank; Iconography; Synaxis of the Archangels
Notes: Not a single named angel but an office/ministry of the ninth rank (the Angels). Core doctrine rooted in Scripture (Matthew 18:10; Acts 12:15; Psalm 90/91:11); the assignment of a personal guardian to each of the faithful is developed in patristic and liturgical tradition. No separate feast — commemorated with all the bodiless powers on Nov 8.
Sources: Matthew 18:10; Psalm 90(91):11–12; Acts 12:15; Hebrews 1:14; St. Basil the Great, Against Eunomius; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Pseudo-Dionysius, On the Celestial Hierarchy; Horologion; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Church in America

Name: Angel of the Lord
Also Known As: The Angel of the Lord; Angel of Yahweh; Malakh YHWH; Messenger of the Lord; Angel of God
Scripture References: Genesis 16:7–13; Genesis 22:11–18; Exodus 3:2–6; Exodus 23:20–23; Numbers 22:22–35; Judges 6:11–24; Judges 13:3–22; 2 Kings 19:35; Zechariah 3:1–7; Matthew 1:20; Matthew 28:2; Luke 1:11; Acts 12:7–11
Brief: A biblical title — “the Messenger of the LORD” (Malakh YHWH) — for the heavenly figure who appears to Hagar, Abraham, Moses, Gideon, and others across the Old Testament, often speaking with the very voice and authority of God. Many Church Fathers understood certain of these appearances as manifestations of the pre-incarnate Word of God, the Son before His Incarnation, though Scripture itself does not state this explicitly.
Tags: Angel of the Lord; Scriptural Angels; Theophany; Christology; Pre-Incarnate Christ; Logos; Old Testament; Burning Bush; Divine Revelation; Covenant History; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers
Notes: A scriptural title/figure, not a distinct named angel like Michael or Gabriel, and not assigned a place in the Nine Orders. The exact title recurs in the New Testament for created messengers. Many Fathers (Justin, Irenaeus, and others) read major Old-Testament appearances as the pre-incarnate Logos; the Church did not dogmatize that every appearance is Christ. Deuterocanonical and extra-biblical angel texts do not name this specific figure, so those columns are left blank. No distinct feast.
Sources: Genesis 16; 22; Exodus 3; 23; Numbers 22; Judges 6; 13; 2 Kings 19; Zechariah 3; Matthew 1; 28; Luke 1; Acts 12; St. Justin Martyr, Dialogue with Trypho; St. Irenaeus of Lyons, Against Heresies; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Genesis; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion; Orthodox Study Bible

Name: Commander of the Lord’s Army
Also Known As: Commander of the Army of the Lord; Captain of the Lord’s Host; Captain of the Lord’s Army; Prince of the Lord’s Host; The Archistrategos before Jericho
Scripture References: Joshua 5:13–15; Joshua 6:1–5; Exodus 3:2–6; Exodus 23:20–23; Numbers 22:22–35; 2 Samuel 24:16–17; 2 Kings 19:35; Daniel 10:13, 21; 12:1; Matthew 26:53; Luke 2:13; Hebrews 1:14
Brief: The heavenly figure with a drawn sword who appears to Joshua near Jericho and declares Himself “commander of the army of the LORD” (Joshua 5:13–15). Scripture gives no name; Orthodox tradition reads Him either as a high-ranking angelic warrior — often associated with the Archangel Michael — or, because Joshua bows and is told to remove his sandals on holy ground, as a theophany of the pre-incarnate Word.
Tags: Commander of the Lord’s Army; Scriptural Angels; Theophany; Christophany; Pre-Incarnate Christ; Archangel Michael; Joshua; Jericho; Angelic Warfare; Lord of Hosts; Old Testament; Bodiless Powers
Notes: A scriptural figure, not a distinct named angel and not assigned a place in the Nine Orders. Sole explicit appearance: Joshua 5:13–15. Traditionally identified either as an angelic commander (often linked to the Archangel Michael) or as a manifestation of the pre-incarnate Logos (the burning-bush parallel of holy ground and removed sandals); Scripture does not settle the question. Deuterocanonical (2 Macc) and extra-biblical angel texts do not name this figure, so those columns are left blank. No distinct feast; closest liturgical context is the Nov 8 Synaxis.
Sources: Joshua 5:13–15; 6:1–5; Exodus 3:2–6; 23:20–23; Numbers 22:22–35; 2 Samuel 24:16–17; 2 Kings 19:35; Daniel 10; Matthew 26:53; Hebrews 1:14; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Study Bible

Name: Angels of the Seven Churches
Also Known As: Angels of the Churches; Seven Angels of Revelation; The Seven Stars; Heavenly Guardians of the Churches; Angels of the Seven Churches of Asia
Brief: The seven “angels” of the churches of Ephesus, Smyrna, Pergamum, Thyatira, Sardis, Philadelphia, and Laodicea, addressed by Christ through St. John the Theologian in Revelation 2–3 and identified as the seven stars in His right hand (Revelation 1:20). Scripture gives no names; Orthodox tradition has read them as the heavenly guardian angels of the local churches, as their bishops or chief pastors, or as both together.
Tags: Angels of the Seven Churches; Collective; Book of Revelation; Seven Stars; Guardian Angels; Ecclesiology; St. John the Theologian; New Testament; Repentance; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Asia Minor
Notes: A collective of seven scriptural “angels” (Revelation 2–3), not named individuals and not assigned a place in the Nine Orders. Identity is deliberately left open in Orthodox tradition: heavenly guardian angels of the churches, or bishops/chief pastors, or both. Deuterocanonical and extra-biblical texts do not name them, so those columns are left blank. No distinct feast; closest liturgical context is the Nov 8 Synaxis and the feast of St. John the Theologian.
Sources: Revelation 1–3; Daniel 10:13, 21; Matthew 18:10; Hebrews 1:14; St. Andrew of Caesarea, Commentary on the Apocalypse; Oecumenius, Commentary on the Apocalypse; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Study Bible; Archbishop Averky, Commentary on the Apocalypse

Name: Cherubim Guarding Eden
Also Known As: Cherubim of Eden; Guardians of Paradise; Guardians of the Tree of Life; Edenic Cherubim; Paradise Guardians
Scripture References: Genesis 3:22–24; Exodus 25:18–22; Exodus 26:1, 31; Numbers 7:89; 1 Kings 6:23–35; Psalm 80:1; Psalm 99:1; Ezekiel 10:1–22; Hebrews 9:5; Revelation 4:6–8
Brief: The cherubim whom God stationed east of the Garden of Eden after the Fall, with a flaming sword turning every way, to guard the way to the Tree of Life (Genesis 3:24). They are Scripture’s first angelic guardians of sacred space — a sign at once of humanity’s exile from Paradise and, as the Church reads it, of the way to the Tree of Life that Christ reopens through His Cross and Resurrection. Orthodox tradition identifies them with the Cherubim, the second rank of the celestial hierarchy.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Cherubim; Garden of Eden; Tree of Life; Paradise; Genesis; Old Testament; Sacred Space; Divine Presence; Protection; Judgment; Salvation History; Guardians of Paradise; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers; Iconography; Patristics
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Cherubim rank (see HH-0002), not a distinct named being; catalogued here among the Biblical Encounters. Genesis 3:24 is the sole passage placing cherubim at Eden; the other cited texts (the Ark, the Tabernacle veil, Solomon’s Temple, Ezekiel’s throne-vision) describe cherubim in general and supply the scriptural context. No deuterocanonical book names the Eden cherubim, so that column is left blank; 1 Enoch and Jubilees — Second Temple literature outside the canon — describe cherubic throne-attendants and Paradise guardians and are recorded as extra-biblical only. No distinct feast; commemorated with all the bodiless powers at the Nov 8 Synaxis (FF-0071). The link drawn between the closing of Paradise here and its reopening through the Cross is a liturgical and patristic theme, framed as such, not a separate doctrine.
Sources: Genesis 3:22–24; Exodus 25:18–22; 26:1, 31; Numbers 7:89; 1 Kings 6:23–35; Psalms 80; 99; Ezekiel 1; 10; 11; Hebrews 9:5; Revelation 4; St. Basil the Great, Hexaemeron; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Genesis; St. Gregory the Theologian, Orations; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Ephrem the Syrian, Hymns on Paradise; St. Maximus the Confessor, Ambigua; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: Jacob’s Ladder
Also Known As: Jacob’s Dream; Vision at Bethel; Ladder to Heaven; Jacob’s Vision; The Ladder of Jacob
Scripture References: Genesis 28:10–22; Genesis 35:1–15; John 1:51; Hebrews 1:14; Daniel 7:10; Revelation 4–5
Brief: The vision granted to the Patriarch Jacob at Bethel as he fled toward Haran (Genesis 28:10–22): a ladder set on the earth with its top reaching to heaven, the angels of God ascending and descending upon it, and the Lord standing above it to renew the covenant of Abraham and Isaac. The Church reads the ladder as a type of Christ, who unites heaven and earth (John 1:51), and of the Theotokos, through whom God descended to dwell among us.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Patriarch Jacob; Angels; Bethel; Biblical Vision; Covenant; Revelation; Divine Providence; Angelic Ministry; Spiritual Ascent; Theotokos Typology; Christological Typology; Salvation History; Ladder of Divine Ascent; Heavenly Hosts; Bodiless Powers
Notes: An event-anchored Old-Testament vision, not a distinct named being; the angels are simply “angels of God,” unnamed and of unspecified rank, so the Celestial Order is left blank (Orthodox tradition does not fix their order). Tobit 12:15 is a thematic parallel on angelic mediation, not a reference to this vision, so the Deuterocanonical column is left blank; the Book of Jubilees retells Jacob’s vision and is recorded as extra-biblical (Second Temple, non-canonical) only. No distinct feast commemorates the event, though its imagery recurs constantly in the hymnography of the Theotokos (“the Ladder of Jacob”) and in the monastic tradition of St. John Climacus. The Christological (John 1:51) and Marian typologies are the interpretation of the Fathers and hymnographers, framed as such.
Sources: Genesis 28:10–22; 35:1–15; John 1:51; Hebrews 1:14; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Genesis; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. Gregory the Theologian, Orations; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. John Climacus, The Ladder of Divine Ascent; Menaion; Akathist Hymn to the Theotokos; Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: The Angel and Hagar
Also Known As: Hagar and the Angel; The Angel of the Lord Appears to Hagar; The Angel of God Calls to Hagar; Hagar in the Wilderness; The Angel at the Spring of Shur; The God Who Sees; Beer-lahai-roi
Scripture References: Genesis 16:1–16; Genesis 21:8–21; Galatians 4:21–31; Hebrews 1:14
Brief: The two wilderness episodes in which the Angel of the Lord comes to the aid of Hagar, the Egyptian servant of Sarah. In Genesis 16 the Angel finds her fleeing beside a spring, sends her back, and announces the birth of Ishmael; in Genesis 21 the Angel of God calls to her from heaven as her child lies dying of thirst and opens her eyes to a well. Hagar names the Lord “the God who sees” — one of Scripture’s most striking scenes of divine care for a vulnerable outsider. The figure is an appearance of the Angel of the Lord, whom the Church reads either as a created messenger speaking in God’s name or, in the great theophanies, as the pre-incarnate Word.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Genesis; Angel of the Lord; Angel of God; Angels; Hagar; Ishmael; Wilderness; Divine Providence; Divine Mercy; Protection; Guidance; Revelation; God Sees; God Hears; Covenant History; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Angel of the Lord (see HH-0019), not a distinct being. The angel is unnamed in Scripture and must not be identified with a named archangel; his rank is unspecified and is treated under the general order of Angels, so the Celestial Order is left blank. Tobit 12:12–15 is a thematic parallel on angelic mediation, not a reference to this event, so the Deuterocanonical column is left blank; the Book of Jubilees retells the Genesis narrative including Hagar and Ishmael and is recorded as extra-biblical (Second Temple, non-canonical) only. Galatians 4:21–31 uses Hagar allegorically but is not about the angel. No distinct feast; general connection to the Nov 8 Synaxis and the commemoration of the Forefathers. Whether the Angel of the Lord here is a created messenger or a theophany of the Word is presented as interpretation, distinguished from the direct biblical account.
Sources: Genesis 16:1–16; 21:8–21; Galatians 4:21–31; Hebrews 1:14; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Genesis; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: The Angel and Abraham
Also Known As: The Angel and the Binding of Isaac; The Angel of the Lord and Abraham; The Akedah; The Sacrifice of Isaac; Abraham on Mount Moriah
Scripture References: Genesis 22:1–18; Hebrews 11:17–19; James 2:21–23; Romans 4:1–25
Brief: The angelic intervention at the Binding of Isaac (Genesis 22, the Akedah). When Abraham, obedient to God’s command, raises the knife to offer his son Isaac upon Mount Moriah, the Angel of the Lord calls from heaven — “Abraham, Abraham! Do not lay your hand on the lad” — halting the sacrifice, providing a ram in Isaac’s place, and renewing the covenant promise. The Church reads Isaac bearing the wood as a type of Christ, the ram as His sacrifice, and Moriah as a foreshadowing of Golgotha; the figure is again the Angel of the Lord, understood either as a created messenger or, by some Fathers, as the pre-incarnate Word.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Genesis; Angel of the Lord; Angels; Abraham; Isaac; Binding of Isaac; Akedah; Mount Moriah; Covenant; Faith; Obedience; Sacrifice; Divine Provision; Typology; Holy Week; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Angel of the Lord (see HH-0019), not a distinct being. The angel is unnamed in Scripture and must not be identified with a named archangel; his rank is unspecified and treated under the general order of Angels, so the Celestial Order is left blank. Sirach 44:19–21 (a deuterocanonical/anagignoskomena text) praises Abraham as found faithful when tested — a genuine witness to this event, though it commends Abraham rather than naming the angel, and is recorded in the Deuterocanonical column on that basis. The Book of Jubilees retells the binding and is recorded as extra-biblical (Second Temple, non-canonical) only. The Isaac-as-Christ, ram-as-sacrifice, and Moriah-as-Golgotha typologies are patristic and liturgical interpretation, framed as such, not the plain sense of Genesis. No distinct feast; Abraham is commemorated among the Forefathers, and the typology is sung on Great and Holy Saturday, where Genesis 22 is a Vesperal reading.
Sources: Genesis 22:1–18; Hebrews 11:17–19; James 2:21–23; Romans 4; Sirach 44:19–21; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies on Genesis; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. Gregory the Theologian, Orations; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Ephrem the Syrian; Menaion; Triodion; Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: The Angel and Balaam
Also Known As: Balaam and the Angel; Balaam’s Donkey and the Angel; The Angel of the Lord Opposes Balaam; Balaam’s Journey; The Drawn Sword of the Angel
Scripture References: Numbers 22:22–35; Numbers 22:1–21; Numbers 23–24; Deuteronomy 23:4–5; Joshua 24:9–10; Nehemiah 13:2; Micah 6:5; 2 Peter 2:15–16; Jude 11; Revelation 2:14
Brief: The confrontation of Numbers 22:22–35, in which the Angel of the Lord stands in the road with a drawn sword to bar the way of the prophet-diviner Balaam, hired by Balak of Moab to curse Israel. The angel is seen first only by Balaam’s donkey, which turns aside three times and is beaten for it, until God gives the animal speech and opens Balaam’s eyes to the sword-bearing angel before him. The encounter shows that even a prophet may be spiritually blind, that humility perceives what pride cannot, and that God’s protection of His covenant people cannot be overturned — Balaam can utter only the blessing God permits.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Numbers; Angel of the Lord; Angels; Balaam; Balak; Drawn Sword; Divine Providence; Judgment; Protection; Revelation; Spiritual Blindness; Humility; Obedience; Prophecy; Covenant; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Angel of the Lord (see HH-0019), not a distinct being. The angel is unnamed in Scripture and must not be identified with a named archangel; his rank is unspecified and treated under the general order of Angels, so the Celestial Order is left blank. Unusually the angel appears as an adversary — he stands “as an adversary” (Hebrew le-satan, to oppose) — yet the opposition is protective, keeping Balaam from acting against God’s will; the drawn-sword figure parallels the Commander of the Lord’s Army before Joshua (HH-0020). No deuterocanonical book names this encounter, so that column is left blank; the Book of Jubilees’ narrative ends at Sinai and does NOT contain the Numbers/Balaam episode, so it is not cited — the chief later development is rabbinic and in Testament literature, portraying Balaam negatively, and carries no canonical authority, so the Extra-Biblical column is left blank as well. No distinct feast; general connection to the Nov 8 Synaxis. Balaam’s oracle “a Star shall come out of Jacob” (Numbers 24:17) — the fruit of the angel’s intervention turning the curse to blessing — is read at the Nativity as a prophecy of the Star of Bethlehem. The theophany-vs-created-messenger question is presented as interpretation, distinguished from the biblical text.
Sources: Numbers 22:22–35; 23–24; Deuteronomy 23:4–5; Joshua 24:9–10; Nehemiah 13:2; Micah 6:5; 2 Peter 2:15–16; Jude 11; Revelation 2:14; St. John Chrysostom, Biblical Homilies; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. Gregory the Theologian, Orations; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Ephrem the Syrian; Menaion; Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: The Burning Bush
Also Known As: The Unconsumed Bush; The Unburnt Bush; Moses at Horeb; The Bush That Burned and Was Not Consumed; The Revelation at Horeb; Moses’ Call Narrative
Scripture References: Exodus 3:1–6; Exodus 3:7–15; Exodus 4:1–17; Deuteronomy 33:16; Mark 12:26; Luke 20:37; Acts 7:30–35; Hebrews 11:23–29
Extra-Biblical Sources: Philo of Alexandria, On the Life of Moses
Brief: The theophany of Exodus 3–4 at Mount Horeb, where Moses, tending Jethro’s flock, sees a bush aflame yet not consumed. The Angel of the Lord appears in the flame and God speaks from the midst of the bush — bidding Moses remove his sandals on holy ground, revealing the divine Name (“I AM WHO I AM”), and commissioning him to lead Israel out of Egypt. The Church reads the bush that bore divine fire unconsumed as the great type of the Theotokos, who bore God incarnate without being consumed — an image at the very heart of Orthodox Marian hymnography and of the “Unburnt Bush” icon.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Exodus; Moses; Angel of the Lord; Theophany; Mount Sinai; Mount Horeb; Divine Revelation; Divine Presence; Holiness; Fire; Liberation; Prophetic Calling; Theotokos Typology; Marian Symbolism; Unburnt Bush; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Angel of the Lord (see HH-0019) and the central Old-Testament theophany: Exodus 3:2 names the Angel of the Lord in the flame, yet God Himself speaks from the bush. The biblical text is presented first and the theophany / pre-incarnate-Word reading named as interpretation (see HH-0019 for the full question). The figure is unnamed and of unspecified rank, treated under the general order of Angels, so the Celestial Order is left blank. No deuterocanonical book names the Burning Bush — Wisdom of Solomon 18 reflects on the Passover deliverance, not this call at Horeb — so the Deuterocanonical column is left blank; Philo of Alexandria (On the Life of Moses) gives an influential allegorical reading of the bush and is recorded as extra-biblical (Hellenistic Jewish) background, while Jubilees does not substantially treat the scene. Unlike most Old-Testament angelic encounters this one is liturgically central: the unconsumed bush is the pre-eminent type of the Theotokos (“the Bush unburnt”), sung across her feasts and imaged in the “Unburnt Bush” icon. No feast commemorates the event itself.
Sources: Exodus 3–4; Deuteronomy 33:16; Mark 12:26; Luke 20:37; Acts 7:30–35; Hebrews 11:23–29; St. Gregory of Nyssa, The Life of Moses; St. Gregory the Theologian, Orations; St. John Chrysostom, Homilies; St. Basil the Great; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; St. Ephrem the Syrian; St. Andrew of Crete; Menaion; Akathist Hymn to the Theotokos; Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: The Destruction of Sennacherib’s Army
Also Known As: The Angel of the Lord Defends Jerusalem; The Angel and Sennacherib’s Army; The Destruction of the Assyrian Army; The Deliverance of Jerusalem; The Defeat of Sennacherib; Hezekiah’s Deliverance
Scripture References: 2 Kings 19:35–37; Isaiah 37:36–38; 2 Chronicles 32:21; 2 Kings 18:13–19:34; Isaiah 36:1–37:35; 2 Chronicles 32:1–20
Extra-Biblical Sources: Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews X
Brief: The angelic judgment of 2 Kings 19:35–37 (with Isaiah 37 and 2 Chronicles 32), in which the Angel of the Lord goes out in the night and strikes the camp of Sennacherib, king of Assyria, besieging Jerusalem in the days of King Hezekiah — 185,000 soldiers fall, and Sennacherib withdraws to Nineveh, where his own sons later kill him. The angel speaks no word; his deed itself is the message. It is among Scripture’s clearest signs of an angel as the instrument of divine judgment and protection — delivering Jerusalem in answer to Hezekiah’s prayer and Isaiah’s prophecy, and preserving the city of David and the Temple.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Angel of the Lord; Angels; Angelic Judgment; Divine Protection; Hezekiah; Prophet Isaiah; Sennacherib; Assyria; Jerusalem; Prayer; Prophecy; Divine Sovereignty; Spiritual Warfare; Deliverance; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: An event-anchored appearance of the Angel of the Lord (see HH-0019), not a distinct being. The angel is unnamed in Scripture and must not be identified with a named archangel; the traditional association with the Archangel Michael, defender of God’s people, is thematic only, not the biblical identification. Rank unspecified, treated under the general order of Angels, so the Celestial Order is left blank. This event is unusually well attested across the source registers: the deuterocanonical Sirach 48:21 says “the Lord struck the camp of the Assyrians, and His angel wiped them out,” and the recollections in 1 Maccabees 7:41 and 2 Maccabees 8:19 invoke the destruction as a precedent for divine deliverance — all recorded in the Deuterocanonical column. Extra-biblically, Josephus retells the episode (Antiquities X); the Assyrian royal inscriptions (Sennacherib’s Prism / Taylor Prism) confirm the campaign and the siege but claim no capture of Jerusalem and say nothing of the deliverance, and Herodotus preserves a later Greek tradition of a plague of mice — historical comparanda, not Orthodox doctrine. No distinct feast; general connection to the Nov 8 Synaxis. Here protection and judgment fall in a single act.
Sources: 2 Kings 18–19; Isaiah 36–37; 2 Chronicles 32; Sirach 48:17–25; 1 Maccabees 7:41; 2 Maccabees 8:19; St. Basil the Great, On the Holy Spirit; St. John Chrysostom (on Hezekiah and prayer); St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; Josephus, Antiquities of the Jews X; Menaion (Nov 8); Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity

Name: Daniel’s Angelic Visions
Also Known As: Daniel’s Heavenly Visions; Daniel’s Angelic Revelations; The Visions of Daniel; Gabriel and Daniel; Michael and Daniel
Scripture References: Daniel 7:9–14; Daniel 7:15–28; Daniel 8:15–26; Daniel 9:20–27; Daniel 10:4–21; Daniel 12:1; Daniel 4:13–17; Daniel 6:22; Daniel 3:24–28; Matthew 24:15; Luke 1:19, 26; Jude 9; Revelation 12:7–9
Deuterocanonical Sources: Song of the Three Holy Children (LXX Daniel 3); Bel and the Dragon (LXX Daniel 14:33–39)
Brief: The sustained cycle of revelations in the Book of Daniel (chiefly chapters 7–12), which gives the Old Testament its most detailed unveiling of the angelic world. Here the Archangel Gabriel appears by name to interpret Daniel’s visions (Daniel 8–9), and the Archangel Michael is named as “one of the chief princes” and “the great prince who stands watch over your people” (Daniel 10; 12). Daniel also beholds the Ancient of Days enthroned amid myriads of heavenly attendants, the radiant messenger of Daniel 10, the “watchers” who announce God’s judgment, and the angelic “princes” set over the nations. The book named Michael and Gabriel long before their appearances in the Gospel and the Apocalypse, and became a wellspring of Orthodox angelology, iconography, and eschatology.
Tags: Biblical Encounter; Old Testament; Prophet Daniel; Archangel Gabriel; Archangel Michael; Angels; Watchers; Ancient of Days; Prophecy; Revelation; Apocalyptic; Spiritual Warfare; Divine Judgment; Heavenly Worship; Angelic Orders; Seventy Weeks; Protection; Salvation History; Bodiless Powers; Patristic Interpretation
Notes: A vision cycle rather than a single event, and — unlike the other Old-Testament encounters — one that turns on NAMED angels (Gabriel HH-0011 and Michael HH-0010), so it anchors to the Archangels rather than to the Angel of the Lord. Entity type Collective (many beings — Gabriel, Michael, the watchers, the heavenly attendants of the Ancient of Days, and the angelic princes of the nations); the Celestial Order is left blank because several orders are involved. The registers are unusually full: within the protocanonical Daniel, Gabriel and Michael are named and the furnace-angel and lions’-den angel appear (Daniel 3; 6); the Greek (deuterocanonical) additions add further angelic passages — the angel of the Lord in the furnace (Song of the Three Holy Children) and the angel who bears Habakkuk to Daniel in the den (Bel and the Dragon) — recorded in the Deuterocanonical column. Extra-biblically, 1 Enoch, Jubilees, and 2 Esdras develop the watchers and the named archangels; note carefully that Daniel’s “watchers” (Daniel 4) are HOLY heavenly beings who announce God’s decree, whereas 1 Enoch uses the term chiefly for the FALLEN angels of Genesis 6 — the two must not be conflated. Daniel 10’s “prince of Persia / prince of Greece” is the scriptural root of the later Orthodox teaching on the Principalities set over the nations (HH-0007). No feast commemorates the cycle itself; the Prophet Daniel is honored among the prophets and forefathers, and Gabriel and Michael on their own feasts.
Sources: Daniel 3–12; Matthew 24:15; Luke 1:19, 26; Jude 9; Revelation 12:7–9; Song of the Three Holy Children (LXX Daniel 3); Bel and the Dragon (LXX Daniel 14); St. Hippolytus of Rome, Commentary on Daniel; St. Jerome, Commentary on Daniel; St. Basil the Great; St. John Chrysostom; St. John of Damascus, An Exact Exposition of the Orthodox Faith; St. Dionysius the Areopagite, On the Celestial Hierarchy; Menaion; Orthodox Study Bible; Metropolitan Hilarion Alfeyev, Orthodox Christianity
